sentences.text: William N.
McKinney.
And you want me to spell it?
W-I-L-L-I-A-M, N as in Nathaniel, McKinney, M-C-K-I-N-N-E-Y.
Bill.
B-I-L-L.
February 1, 1923.
I was born in the state of Pennsylvania, in Uniontown.
U-I-O-N-T-O-W-N.
In the state of Pennsylvania.
Country, USA.
I grew up in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.
And I come from a family of six boys and four girls.
My parents-- my mother was a housekeeper in our home.
And my father, he worked various jobs.
He was-- he served in World War I.
And he was gassed.
And he worked various jobs to try to raise the family.
Well, he fought in the trenches in France with the 377th Infantry Division.
And during World War I, they were using different types of gas, mainly mustard gas.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as a consequence, he was gassed in those trenches.
He survived.
And he lived to be 94.
I said he was gassed.
And he-- when he died, he died with some sort of kidney failure.
I was brought away from Uniontown when I was 10 months old, so it'd be very difficult for me to describe it.
Pittsburgh.
Well, it was compatible.
We had a home that was full of love.
And we were all hard workers.
And we had time for play-- play and work.
Well, I was next to the baby boy.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I had three other brothers older than I.
And can you describe your-- your childhood, what kind of things you did when you were growing boy in Pittsburgh?
Well, we'd go out in the woods.
We lived in Penn Township.
Penn Hills, today they call it.
My baby brother and I'd go out in the woods and pick elderberries, apples, and so forth.
My mother would make elderberry jelly, and apple jelly, and apple sauce, and so forth.
And--
It was a large house.
Oh.
[PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]
Well, it was a mixed neighborhood.
Well, it was both black and white families.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Well, the normal Christian holidays.
Christmas, Easter, Fourth of July-- Independence Day-- Memorial Day.
It was racially mixed.
Oh, it would've been 1939, about '41.
Well, when I went to high school, I wasn't no A student, but just a mediocre student.
And the thing that fascinated me most was the sciences, biology and-- and physics.
I enjoyed those studies the most.
No.
Well, my baby brother and I, we would play together.
Not when I was a youngster, no.
Well, I went off to the military.
That was in 1943.
Yes.
I was 18, turning 19.
Yes.
Well, I was drafted.
My family had moved from Penn Township to Stanley Heights district, off of-- on Centre Avenue.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was drafted from [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Draft Board number five at Herron Hill Junior High School.
And that's where I was drafted into the military, June 6, 1943.
Well, I didn't experience anything out of the ordinary.
It was racially mixed and balanced.
And--
Racially mixed and balanced.
Yes.
No.
Well, the day that I was drafted, there was many young men down at the draft board downtown at the old post office building in downtown Pittsburgh.
At the old post office building.
And there were-- oh, about-- a little over 300 men there, young men.
And they're-- oh, they're ranging in age from 18 to 25.
Well, they gave us various tests and so forth.
And one test in particular that they gave was a communication.
And I scored the highest out of all the men.
And it was Morse code.
And then they put me in the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] air base security battalion--
--to transmit-- that was at Camp Rucker, Alabama.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I said goodbye to my mother and dad, and my-- there was two other brothers and myself.
We went to downtown to the B and O station to ship out for various designations.
And my fiancee, she was there, along with her sister.
She was a twin.
And she [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] saw me off.
And I told her-- I said, I will get married, but it wouldn't be fair to my mother, and because a-- on induction, they got-- each soldier was insured for $10,000.
And I said, if anything happened to me, we would have not been married long enough for your entitlement to this $10,000.
And I said, my mother has done more for me up to this point.
So I named my mother as the beneficiary if anything should happen to me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, as I promised her, after the war, we got married.
And from the marriage, we had three sons.
Yes, I did.
Well, I arrived at Fort Meade, Maryland.
And I got the necessary inoculation shots.
And then from Fort Meade, I was shipped to Camp Rucker, Alabama.
And my other brothers, one was sent to-- Fort Belvoir, Virginia, do and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my baby brother was sent to [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a camp in Michigan.
I don't remember just what the name of the camp was.
Well, at first, the outfit that I was in, the lowest rank was corporal technician.
They had all technical corporal sergeants, and master sergeant, and first sergeant.
And I was put under-- they-- they gave us a test again.
And they had narrowed it down to 76 men.
And out of the 76, I scored the highest again on communication.
Morse code.
Then they [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] put me under the direct [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] learning techniques of a Sergeant Younger.
It's more or less he was going to teach me the ins and outs of Morse code.
And I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I started it-- well, the bigger part of my days were spent with him, learning the ins and outs of Morse code.
This was part of my basic training.
Then they had these air base security battalions in North Africa.
And two or three of them, they got wiped completely out.
And see, air base security battalion was similar to the infantry.
They were supposed to guard the airfields.
And so they broke the air base security battalion up and transferred us all into the quartermaster.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's-- from there, they-- I was only at Camp Rucker about a month.
Alabama.
Then they sent us to Camp Shelby, Mississippi.
And it was a hellhole.
That's where I began to experience a great deal of prejudice, and indifference, and so forth, and name-calling and so forth.
Well, when they hauled us in trucks, when the trucks pulled up on the base, I saw these beautiful white barracks.
And I saw in-- you know, manicured lawns.
I thought, this is a nice camp.
They drove by the barracks and took us down to the back end of the camp.
And they had these little 4-by-4 black huts.
No windows.
They had [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] panels that they would raise and lower.
You know, it-- it was screened.
When it was hot, you just raised it during the day.
And if it was a cool night, you'd lower it.
And I think they slept six to a barrack.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we encountered some difficulties with the white civilians and the white soldiers.
Well, there was name-calling.
And-- and then there were fights that broke out.
And--
Well, they would call us niggers and blackie and all sorts of--
Blackies.
Nigger.
And all sorts of name-calling.
And spit at us and so forth.
White soldiers.
See, the soldiers weren't integrated then.
They had all-black outfits and a white outfit.
And so we have a colonel.
His name was Colonel Castle.
He was a battalion commander.
He was a white man.
Oh, he treated us-- he was a good man.

And so-- because Colonel Castle, he was the one that was responsible to have our outfit transferred out of Camp Shelby, Mississippi, because he told Washington, DC-- the high command in Washington-- told them if they didn't ship us out of there, he would not be held responsible for what would happen.
He was going to arm the battalion.
He was going to arm us-- arm the battalion and let them go up against these white soldiers.
So I was only there three weeks.
Then they shipped us to Camp Forrest, Tennessee.
That's where I completed my basic training, at Camp Forrest, Tennessee.
And how was the treatment to you as being black American there?
At Camp Forrest?
It was somewhat similar, but it wasn't as bad as it was in Mississippi.
The-- you encountered a lot of prejudice in the South-- well, at Tennessee, Alabama, Georgia.
All the states where they had these camps.
They were segregated.
Yes.
You get passes and go into town.
And at Camp Forrest, you had [INAUDIBLE] you-- you could go to, or Memphis.
Well, generally as a rule, we would find black communities.
And we were treated well.
And a lot of places I had read about when I was in school-- when I'd go on pass, my chief interest was to see these places and visit these places, like Meharry.
It was a black university there in Tennessee, in Nashville.
And I'd go out there, meet with different persons.
And then there was a Jackson [INAUDIBLE].
I'd go there and have lunch.
And stayed at the YMCA.
Oh, well, in the white community, they had their restaurants.
And in the black community, they had their restaurants.
No.
No.
Most of the restaurants that I frequented when I went into town were black.
